title: How loneliness changes the way our brains process the world

passage.title: How loneliness changes the way our brains process the world
passage.author: By Robin Kramer
passage.text:
Originally published in [*The Conversation*](https://theconversation.com/how-loneliness-changes-the-way-our-brains-process-the-world-214668).

1. If there’s one thing we as humans seem to have in common, it’s that most of us have felt lonely at one time or another. But is the pain that comes with feeling socially isolated simply a part of being human? Why does the world seem so different when we’re feeling lonely?

2. Recent research has begun to provide some answers. And it turns out that loneliness can affect your [word] and [word].

3. Although no one enjoys the feeling of loneliness, scientists have argued that humans evolved to feel this way for good reason.

4. Social relationships are crucial, providing safety, resources, opportunities to have children, and so on. The fact that we find the feeling of loneliness so unpleasant often motivates us to reconnect with others, bringing with it all of these benefits.

5. But it’s not as simple as that. Feeling lonely can also __induce__ social withdrawal and types of negative thinking, which can make it harder to connect with people.

###The lonely brain

6. Studies have identified differences in brain areas associated with loneliness. In lonely young adults, areas of the brain related to social cognition and [word] have less dense white matter (a large network of nerve fibers that allows the exchange of information and communication between different areas of your brain). But in lonely older adults, brain regions important for cognitive processing and emotional regulation are actually smaller in volume.

7. A recent study found that the brains of lonely people process the world [word]. The researchers asked participants to watch a series of video clips while inside an  [word] scanner and found that non-lonely people showed very similar [word] activity to each other, whereas lonely people showed brain activity that was dissimilar to each other and to the non-lonely participants. So lonely people appear to view the world differently to others.

###Finding friends in fiction

8. This is also evident in how lonely people view fictional characters. Researchers in the U.S. carried out brain scans on fans of the television series *Game of Thrones* while these fans decided whether various adjectives accurately described characters from the show. The authors of the study were able to identify activity in the brain that distinguished between real and fictional people.

9. While the difference between these two categories was clear in non-lonely people, the boundary was blurred for lonelier people. These results suggest that feeling lonely may be associated with thinking of fictional characters in a way similar to real-world friends.

10. However, given the design of the study, it’s unclear whether the findings suggest loneliness causes this way of thinking or if considering fictional characters in this way causes people to feel lonely. And there’s always the possibility that a third factor causes both outcomes.

11. Another recent study, this time by researchers in Scotland, provided more evidence of how loneliness can affect your cognition. This study focused on [word] objects. Participants were shown images of products containing pareidolic faces (face-like patterns) and were asked to give several ratings like how eager they were to explore the product and how likely they were to buy it.

12. The results showed that lonelier participants (compared with those lower in self-reported loneliness) were more likely to attend to, engage with, and purchase products that showed “happy” [word]. These findings may again provide evidence that loneliness is associated with the drive to find connection, even if it’s with objects.

13. Indeed, this makes sense in the light of previous work showing that lonelier people are more likely to [word] gadgets or their own pets.

14. If we look at these studies and what they seem to be telling us, loneliness is not only the perceived absence of others, but also a desire for connection. Whether that’s thinking of fictional characters like real friends or being drawn to happy objects, our brains seem to search for social connections wherever they might find them, especially when we don’t feel like other humans are providing enough of these for us.

questions.question_content: **What claim does the author make about how loneliness affects the way people view the world?**

choices.label: A
choices.content: Loneliness has little to no effect on our perception of the world.

choices.label: B
choices.content: Our views of the world are entirely dependent on our sense of loneliness.

choices.label: C
choices.content: Loneliness significantly affects how we perceive the world around us.

choices.label: D
choices.content: Other people’s views of the world impact whether we feel loneliness.

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
